request.question: When was the winner of the series of international poker tournaments started in 2002 by Steven Lipscomb who has also won four World Series of Poker bracelets born?

title: World Series of Poker Europe
World Series of Poker bracelet
World Poker Tour
World Series of Poker Africa
World Series of Poker Asia Pacific
Gene Fisher
Phil Hellmuth
John Hennigan (poker player)
Michael Gathy
World Poker Tour bracelet
sentences: The World Series of Poker Europe (WSOPE) is the first expansion effort of World Series of Poker-branded poker tournaments outside the United States.
Since 1970, participants had to travel to Las Vegas if they wanted to compete in the World Series of Poker (WSOP).
Although the WSOP held circuit events in other locations, the main tournaments, which awarded bracelets to the winners, were exclusively held in Las Vegas.
The inaugural WSOPE, held in 2007, marked the first time that a WSOP bracelet was awarded outside of Las Vegas.

From its inception to the 2013 tournament, players from 19 countries — USA (10), France (4), UK (3), Denmark (3), Canada (2), Norway (2), Portugal (2), Italy (2), Afghanistan, Germany, Indonesia, Spain, New Zealand, Sweden, Tunisia, Switzerland, Australia, Italy and Finland — have won bracelets.
The World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelet is considered the most coveted non-monetary prize a poker player can win.
Since 1976, a bracelet has been awarded to the winner of every event at the annual WSOP.
Even if the victory occurred before 1976, WSOP championships are now counted as "bracelets".
During the first years of the WSOP only a handful of bracelets were awarded each year.
In 1990, there were only 14 bracelet events.
By 2000, that number increased to 24.
As the popularity of poker has increased during the 2000s, the number of events has likewise increased.
In 2011, 58 bracelets were awarded at the WSOP, seven at the World Series of Poker Europe (WSOPE), and one to the WSOP National Circuit Champion.
This brought the total number of bracelets awarded up to 959.
Five additional bracelets were awarded for the first time in April 2013 at the inaugural World Series of Poker Asia-Pacific (WSOP APAC) in Melbourne, Australia.
In 2017, 74 for bracelets were awarded at the WSOP and an additional 11 will be awarded at the WSOPE in Czech Republic.
The World Poker Tour (WPT) is a series of international poker tournaments and associated television series broadcasting the final table of each tournament.

It was started in 2002 in the United States by attorney/television producer Steven Lipscomb, who served as CEO of WPT Enterprises (WPTE), the firm that controlled the World Poker Tour up to November 2009.
In 2008, the WPT started offering bracelets to its event champions.
Players who won a title prior to the release of the WPT Bracelet were given one retroactively.
The World Series of Poker Africa (WSOPA) is the second expansion effort of World Series of Poker-branded poker tournaments outside the United States.
Since 1970, participants had to travel to Las Vegas if they wanted to compete in the World Series of Poker (WSOP).
Although the WSOP started holding circuit events in 2005 in other states, the main tournaments, which awarded bracelets to the winners, were exclusively held in Las Vegas.
In 2007, the inaugural World Series of Poker Europe marked the first time that a WSOP bracelet was awarded outside of Las Vegas.
In 2010, the WSOP expanded overseas once again, only this time to Gauteng, South Africa.
While the WSOPE awarded bracelets, the WSOPA was considered a circuit event with the winners receiving Championship Rings instead of bracelets.
The WSOPA did not occur in 2011, but the series of tournaments resumed in 2012.
The World Series of Poker Asia Pacific (WSOP APAC) is the third expansion of the World Series of Poker-branded poker tournaments outside the United States.
Since 1970 participants have had to travel to Las Vegas, Nevada to compete in the World Series of Poker (WSOP).
Although the WSOP started holding circuit events in 2005 in other states, the main tournaments, which awarded bracelets to the winners, were exclusively held in Las Vegas.
In 2007 the inaugural World Series of Poker Europe marked the first time that a WSOP bracelet was awarded outside Las Vegas.
The WSOP further expanded to Africa in 2010 and 2012 however these events did not award any bracelets.
Gene Fisher is an American poker player from El Paso, Texas who won World Series of Poker bracelets in 1980 and 1993, winning the same amount ($113,400) in both events.
He has also cashed in various other poker tournaments.
Fisher stopped playing in the World Series of Poker for many years due to problems with the IRS.
Phillip Jerome Hellmuth Jr. (born July 16, 1964) is an American professional poker player who has won a record fourteen World Series of Poker bracelets.

He is the winner of the Main Event of the 1989 World Series of Poker (WSOP) and the Main Event of the 2012 World Series of Poker Europe (WSOPE), and he is a 2007 inductee of the WSOP's Poker Hall of Fame.
Hellmuth is also known for his temperamental "poker brat" personality.

John Hennigan (born August 10, 1970) is an American professional poker player from Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, who, in his career, has won four World Series of Poker bracelets and a World Poker Tour (WPT) title.
Michael Gathy is a professional poker player who won World Series of Poker bracelets at the 2012 and 2013 World Series of Poker.
He was the first World Series turbo event winner and the second Belgian bracelet winner.
He is one of two Belgians with multiple bracelets.
The World Poker Tour bracelet is the World Poker Tour's (WPT) answer to the World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelet.
Beginning in 1976, the WSOP started awarding bracelets to winners of WSOP events.
The WSOP bracelet has become synonymous with greatness.

"It's impossible to overstate the value of a World Series of Poker gold bracelet to anyone who takes the game seriously," stated World Series of Poker Commissioner Jeffrey Pollack during the 2006 bracelet unveiling.
"It is the equivalent of winning the Stanley Cup in hockey or the Lombardi Trophy in football."
Since their introduction, a poker player's success has been measured by the number of bracelets they had won.
With introduction of the WPT bracelet, the WPT hopes to capitalize on the prestige of winning poker bracelets.

WPT Founder, President and CEO, Steve Lipscomb said, "The championship bracelet has become synonymous with poker as a symbol of achievement and respect, and we are honored to continue the tradition that Benny Binion [the founder of the WSOP] began over 30 years ago."

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: When was the winner of the series of international poker tournaments started in 2002 by Steven Lipscomb who has also won four World Series of Poker bracelets born?
hop1_docs: World Poker Tour | The World Poker Tour (WPT) is a series of international poker tournaments and associated television series broadcasting the final table of each tournament.  It was started in 2002 in the United States by attorney/television producer Steven Lipscomb, who served as CEO of WPT Enterprises (WPTE), the firm that controlled the World Poker Tour up to November 2009.  In 2008, the WPT started offering bracelets to its event champions.  Players who won a title prior to the release of the WPT Bracelet were given one retroactively.

World Poker Tour bracelet | The World Poker Tour bracelet is the World Poker Tour's (WPT) answer to the World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelet.  Beginning in 1976, the WSOP started awarding bracelets to winners of WSOP events.  The WSOP bracelet has become synonymous with greatness.  "It's impossible to overstate the value of a World Series of Poker gold bracelet to anyone who takes the game seriously," stated World Series of Poker Commissioner Jeffrey Pollack during the 2006 bracelet unveiling.  "It is the equivalent of winning the Stanley Cup in hockey or the Lombardi Trophy in football."  Since their introduction, a poker player's success has been measured by the number of bracelets they had won.  With introduction of the WPT bracelet, the WPT hopes to capitalize on the prestige of winning poker bracelets.  WPT Founder, President and CEO, Steve Lipscomb said, "The championship bracelet has become synonymous with poker as a symbol of achievement and respect, and we are honored to continue the tradition that Benny Binion [the founder of the WSOP] began over 30 years ago."

David Chiu (poker player) | David Chiu (; born August 23, 1960 in Nanning, China) is a Chinese American professional poker player, based in Las Vegas, Nevada, who has won five World Series of Poker bracelets.  He is also the winner of the 2008 World Poker Tour's WPT World Championship, and the first winner of the Tournament of Champions of Poker.

World Series of Poker Africa | The World Series of Poker Africa (WSOPA) is the second expansion effort of World Series of Poker-branded poker tournaments outside the United States.  Since 1970, participants had to travel to Las Vegas if they wanted to compete in the World Series of Poker (WSOP).  Although the WSOP started holding circuit events in 2005 in other states, the main tournaments, which awarded bracelets to the winners, were exclusively held in Las Vegas.  In 2007, the inaugural World Series of Poker Europe marked the first time that a WSOP bracelet was awarded outside of Las Vegas.  In 2010, the WSOP expanded overseas once again, only this time to Gauteng, South Africa.  While the WSOPE awarded bracelets, the WSOPA was considered a circuit event with the winners receiving Championship Rings instead of bracelets.  The WSOPA did not occur in 2011, but the series of tournaments resumed in 2012.

George Danzer | George Danzer (born 17 July 1983 in São Paulo, Brazil) is a German professional poker player, based in Salzburg, Austria who is a four-time World Series of Poker bracelets winner, winning the inaugural $10,000 Seven Card Razz Championship and then the $10,000 Seven-Card Stud High-Low Championship at the 2014 World Series of Poker.  He won his third bracelet winning the $5,000 Mixed Event 8-Game event at the 2014 World Series of Poker Asia Pacific.  His total live tournament winnings exceed $1.9 million

Michael Gathy | Michael Gathy is a professional poker player who won World Series of Poker bracelets at the 2012 and 2013 World Series of Poker.  He was the first World Series turbo event winner and the second Belgian bracelet winner.  He is one of two Belgians with multiple bracelets.

World Series of Poker multiple bracelet winners | Below is a list of all poker players who have won multiple World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelets, together with the year(s) in which the bracelets were won.

response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "World Poker Tour" — "started in 2002" and "Steven Lipscomb" (passage [1]: "started in 2002" / "Steven Lipscomb").
  - David Chiu — "born August 23, 1960" and "has won five World Series of Poker bracelets" and "winner of the 2008 World Poker Tour's WPT World Championship" (passage [3]: quotes: "born August 23, 1960"; "won five World Series of Poker bracelets"; "winner of the 2008 World Poker Tour's WPT World Championship").
  - George Danzer — "born 17 July 1983" and "four-time World Series of Poker bracelets winner" (passage [5]: quotes: "born 17 July 1983"; "four-time World Series of Poker bracelets winner").
- Ambiguities:
  - The passages present two relevant candidates but do not show a single person with both attributes: David Chiu is a WPT winner but has five WSOP bracelets; George Danzer has four WSOP bracelets but the passages do not state he is a World Poker Tour winner. Do not assume which is intended.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity candidates (as named in the passages): "David Chiu" and "George Danzer" (and the series "World Poker Tour").
  - Precise missing fact needed: identification of the person who both won a World Poker Tour event (a WPT title) and "has won four World Series of Poker bracelets," and that person's birth date.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open the World Poker Tour winners/champions list (page titled "World Poker Tour" or "List of World Poker Tour champions") — check lede/infobox and winners list for any champion who is "George Danzer" or other four-time WSOP bracelet winners.
     - Search keywords: "World Poker Tour winners list", "WPT champions list".
  2) Open "George Danzer" page — check lede/infobox and Career/Wins sections to confirm any WPT titles and verify birth date (lede/infobox already shows birth date in passage but confirm WPT wins).
     - Search keywords: "George Danzer WPT win", check "Career" or "Titles" sections.
  3) Open "David Chiu" page — verify birth date and confirm WSOP bracelet count vs. WPT titles (passage shows he has 5 bracelets and is a 2008 WPT World Championship winner).
     - Search keywords: "David Chiu WPT World Championship 2008", check lede/infobox.
  4) If needed, open "World Series of Poker multiple bracelet winners" page to cross-check players who have exactly four bracelets and then cross-reference those names against WPT winners.
     - Search keywords: "players with four WSOP bracelets WPT winners".
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.

The WSOP further expanded to Africa in 2010 and 2012 however these events did not award any bracelets.

Phil Laak | Philip "Phil" Courtney Laak is an Irish professional poker player and a poker commentator, now residing in Los Angeles, California.  Laak holds a World Poker Tour (WPT) title, a World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelet, and has appeared on numerous nationally aired television shows.
